svg.text: WealthPro — How a Request Travels Through the System
Follow the numbered arrows ① → ⑩ to trace one complete request end-to-end

g.text: ZONE 2 — API GATEWAY (:9999) · the single front door
Resolves logical name → live service instance
Forward to target service (with X-Auth-* headers)
ZONE 3 — 9 MICROSERVICES (each does ONE thing, owns ONE database)
Each reads X-Auth-* headers → enforces ownership via AuthContext
Owns: what you can invest in
JPA / Hibernate query (service reads/writes its own DB)
ZONE 4 — MYSQL DATABASES (one schema per service, no cross-schema joins)
Response travels back: Service → Gateway → User
REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Full Client Lifecycle — 13 Steps in Order
IN PLAIN ENGLISH — what happens when (e.g.) a CLIENT opens their dashboard
1. Browser sends GET /api/analytics/5/dashboard with JWT token.
2. Gateway verifies JWT → role is CLIENT → URL pattern allowed.
3. Gateway strips Authorization header, adds X-Auth-Username, X-Auth-Roles, X-Auth-Client-Id=5, writes audit row.
4. Gateway asks Eureka where ANALYTICS-SERVICE is running → forwards call.
5. Analytics Service reads headers → builds AuthContext → sees user is CLIENT with clientId=5.
6. Controller: "are you trying to read client 5's data?" → yes, caller IS client 5 → allowed.
7. Service queries its analytics schema via JPA → returns JSON → Gateway relays it back to browser.

svg.text: WealthPro Backend • Spring Boot 3.5.13 • Java 24 • 11 microservices • Read arrows in number order
